feed.title: Combined summary - State of the transaction privacy work in Bitcoin

entry.title: Combined summary - State of the transaction privacy work in Bitcoin
entry.summary: The ongoing discussion and technological advancements in Bitcoin privacy focus on enhancing transaction confidentiality through various layers and methods rather than making changes directly to the blockchain protocol itself. Innovations such as Confidential Transactions are being implemented on sidechains, which allow for additional features without altering the main blockchain. Wallet integrations like Silent Payments, PayJoin, and CoinJoin represent key developments that enhance user privacy by masking transaction details and methodologies at the point of interaction, i.e., within users' wallets. The integration of technologies such as Cross-Input Signature Aggregation (CISA) aims to reduce transaction costs associated with privacy solutions like CoinJoin, though it does not directly increase privacy levels. The broader trend in Bitcoin's approach to privacy emphasizes optimizing and building upon existing measures, with the Lightning Network gaining popularity due to its combined offer of enhanced scalability and privacy. CoinSwap represents another significant advancement in the field, providing a mechanism for enhancing on-chain privacy. Its development and potential applications are detailed across various resources, including blog posts and discussion forums. For instance, interested individuals can explore detailed explanations and the current progress of CoinSwap through resources like [Developing Advancements in Onchain Privacy](https://opensats.org/blog/developing-advancements-in-onchain-privacy) and [CoinSwap Topics](https://opensats.org/topics/coinswap). The practical implementations and further specifications of this protocol are actively developed and shared, notably by the Citadel technology team, which provides access to their work via GitHub at [Citadel Tech's CoinSwap](https://github.com/citadel-tech/coinswap) and [Protocol Specification](https://github.com/citadel-tech/Coinswap-Protocol-Specification). Additionally, the use of physical devices such as Opendime and Satscard introduces a means of conducting offline Bitcoin transactions, akin to cash exchanges. These devices play a crucial role by enabling secure, direct transfers without requiring internet connectivity, thereby catering to users prioritizing privacy or those without reliable online access. Further information on these devices is available on their respective websites: [Opendime](https://opendime.com/) and [Satscard](https://satscard.com/). The proposal titled "Shielded CSV," documented at [eprint.iacr.org/2025/068](https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/068), suggests enhancements to trustless interoperability between blockchain systems with a focus on preserving privacy and security through shielded transactions. This proposed soft fork compatible with BIP-441 could potentially lead to more secure and private inter-blockchain transactions, emphasizing the ongoing need for advanced security features that do not compromise the decentralized ethos of cryptocurrencies. Furthermore, the decentralization of coinjoin coordination through platforms like nostr is an emerging topic, addressing regulatory pressures and enhancing transaction privacy without central oversight. Detailed information on this initiative can be found at [joinstr](https://share.google/MhV0i0CeMvv1x0RnB). This development, along with underutilized concepts within the Lightning Network, signifies a broader movement towards improving privacy and efficiency in cryptocurrency transactions, showcasing the dynamic evolution of privacy-enhancing technologies in the digital currency space.
